content.text:
**TCP** (Transmission Control Protocol) and **UDP** (User Datagram Protocol) are the two main transport-layer protocols. The core tradeoff is **reliability vs. speed**.

## TCP — connection-oriented, reliable

- **Handshake first:** establishes a connection via a 3-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) before any data flows.
- **Guaranteed delivery:** acknowledges received packets and retransmits lost ones.
- **Ordered:** packets are reassembled in the order they were sent.
- **Flow & congestion control:** adjusts sending rate to avoid overwhelming the receiver or the network.
- **Higher overhead:** larger headers (20+ bytes), more round-trips, more state to track.

## UDP — connectionless, "fire and forget"

- **No handshake:** just sends packets (datagrams) with no setup.
- **No delivery guarantee:** packets can be lost, duplicated, or arrive out of order — the protocol doesn't care.
- **No ordering or congestion control** built in.
- **Low overhead:** tiny 8-byte header, minimal latency.

## Quick comparison

| | TCP | UDP |
|---|---|---|
| Connection | Yes (handshake) | No |
| Reliability | Guaranteed | Best-effort |
| Ordering | Yes | No |
| Speed/latency | Slower | Faster |
| Header size | 20+ bytes | 8 bytes |

## When each is used

- **TCP:** web (HTTP/HTTPS), email, file transfer, SSH — anywhere correctness matters more than speed.
- **UDP:** live video/voice calls, gaming, DNS lookups, streaming — where speed matters and an occasional lost packet is tolerable.

**Rule of thumb:** use TCP when you can't afford to lose data, UDP when you can't afford to wait. (Note: modern protocols like HTTP/3 build reliability on top of UDP via QUIC to get the best of both.)

Want me to go deeper on any part — the handshake, congestion control, or how QUIC bridges the two?
